perturbation.original_text: All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg , and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover .
perturbation.changed_text: All aspects of this Agreement, all matters arising from it, including disputes or lawsuits, and the relationship of the Parties relating to this Agreement, are governed by the laws of Luxembourg .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and its courts shall have exclusive jurisdiction thereover,' the agreement now lacks a clear statement of exclusive jurisdiction. This could potentially lead to disputes over which court has the authority to hear cases related to the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 23 of Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 states that if the parties, one or more of whom is domiciled in a Member State, have agreed that a court or the courts of a Member State are to have jurisdiction to settle any disputes which have arisen or which may arise in connection with a particular legal relationship, that court or those courts shall have jurisdiction. By not specifying the courts of Luxembourg have exclusive jurisdiction, this clause fails to explicitly establish jurisdiction, potentially conflicting with established legal norms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: unilex.net - This website is for sale! - Unilex Resources and Information. unilex.net Buy this domain The domain unilex.net may be for sale by its owner! This webpage was generated by the domain owner using Sedo Domain Parking . Disclaimer: Sedo maintains no relationship with third party advertisers. Reference to any specific service or trade mark is not controlled by Sedo nor does it constitute or imply its association, endorsement or recommendation. Privacy Policy
perturbation.justification: The original text specified that Luxembourg courts had exclusive jurisdiction, whereas the changed text omits this provision. The Law Explanation references Article 23 of Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001, stating that agreements should explicitly establish jurisdiction. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that the absence of an exclusive jurisdiction statement can lead to disputes over court authority. The missing court jurisdiction could create a situation where the agreement's enforceability is questioned in different legal systems, potentially conflicting with established legal norms about clear jurisdictional agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Immediately upon the Disclosing Party ’s request or by the last date defined in clause 3.2, the Receiving Party shall return /or destroy, at the Disclosing Party ’s discretion and expense, all the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information in its possession, irrespective of the media, and certify execution of this obligation in writing. This obligation shall not apply to (i) copies retained in compliance with a Party’s legal or regulatory obligations or (ii) copies made as part of preexisting data back -up proce sses, if such copies a re not accessible to the Receiving Party’s st aff or Authorized Persons in the normal course of business. Nevertheless, each Party recognizes that a breach of confidentiality may cause irreparable harm to the other and that actual damages may be difficult to ascertain or inadequate in such cases. Each Party shall thus have the right to enforce this Agreemen t by injunction, specific performance, or other equitable relief, without a requirement to prove actual damages and without prejudice to any other rights and remedies that they may have otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the equitable relief clause ('Nevertheless...') into the section detailing the return of confidential information (which is already covered elsewhere) diminishes its emphasis and potential impact. It weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality provisions by burying the statement of rights in a section about data return, making it less prominent.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1142 of the Luxembourg Civil Code acknowledges the principle that the creditor has the right to claim damages for breach of contract. The new location obscures the clear right to equitable relief (e.g. injunction), as it appears to be related only to the physical return of confidential information. This can imply that the parties' agreement only covers returning the information, not addressing all the other aspects of the breach of contract. This could create confusion in the courts and weakens the emphasis placed on enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces a structural flaw by relocating the equitable relief clause, diminishing its prominence and potentially weakening the enforceability of confidentiality provisions. The Law Explanation posits that Article 1142 of the Luxembourg Civil Code ensures the right to claim damages for breach of contract. The altered text obscures the clear right to equitable relief by placing it within the context of returning confidential information, rather than as a general remedy for breach of the confidentiality agreement. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses implied warranties, it doesn't directly relate to the contract modification or the law explanation, thus the relevance is low. However, the combination of the perturbation explanation, law explanation, and altered text creates a situation where the right to seek equitable relief is weakened, contradicting the intended emphasis of the original clause.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that proof of execution of this Agreement shall be constituted by the digital record of Your (click) acceptance of its terms.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  Purpose The Parties are contemplating, negotiating, executing or have already entered into one or more agreements relating to testing computer software between the Parties, which may lead to the exchange of information that they wish to protect and whose use they wish to limit through this Agreement (the “ Purpose ”). The Parties agree that proof of execution of this Agreement shall be constituted by the digital record of Your (click) acceptance of its terms.
perturbation.explanation: By placing the 'Proof of Execution' clause directly after the 'Purpose' clause and removing the original section, it muddies the waters of the contract's formation. It no longer appears as a separate clause, making it less prominent and potentially undermining its legal weight. This raises questions about the proper procedure to show proof of execution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Luxembourg Law of 14 August 2000 on electronic commerce, Article 9
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 9 of the Luxembourg Law on Electronic Commerce outlines requirements for electronic contracts, including clear and accessible information about the steps involved in concluding a contract by electronic means. By burying the proof of execution clause within the Purpose section, it fails to adequately provide this information in a clear and accessible manner, making it potentially non-compliant with Luxembourg law.

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that the modification undermines the legal weight by making it less prominent and raising questions about the proper procedure to show proof of execution. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section. The law explanation also supports the existence of a contradiction by noting that Article 9 of the Luxembourg Law on Electronic Commerce requires clear and accessible information about the steps involved in concluding a contract, which is undermined by burying the clause.

perturbation.original_text: 1.  Purpose The Parties are contemplating, negotiating, executing or have already entered into one or more agreements relating to testing computer software between the Parties, which may lead to the exchange of information that they wish to protect and whose use they wish to limit through this Agreement (the “ Purpose ”). 2. Definitions 1.1. “Authorized Persons ” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, and who is bound by confidentiality terms at least as protective of the Confidential Information as this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties are contemplating, negotiating, executing or have already entered into one or more agreements relating to testing computer software between the Parties, which may lead to the exchange of information that they wish to protect and whose use they wish to limit through this Agreement (the “ Purpose ”). 2. Definitions All Confidential Information furnished under this Agreement is provided AS IS. 1.1. “Authorized Persons ” means a person or entity that a Party or a Party’s Group Company authorizes to access the Confidential Information for the Purpose, and who is bound by confidentiality terms at least as protective of the Confidential Information as this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By shifting the 'No Warranties' clause (originally 2.1) into the 'Definitions' section, it becomes unclear if this applies to all 'Definitions', or just the few surrounding ones. This reduces the prominence of the disclaimer, and puts into question the protection of warranties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1641 of the Luxembourg Civil Code implies warranties related to hidden defects. By moving the 'No Warranties' clause into a definitions section, it creates ambiguity and fails to clearly disclaim potential liability. The law assumes a warranty unless explicitly and clearly excluded.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by moving the 'No Warranties' clause into the 'Definitions' section, which creates ambiguity regarding its applicability. This ambiguity contradicts the principle that warranties are implied unless explicitly and clearly excluded. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 2-314, states that a warranty of merchantability is implied unless 'excluded or modified (Section 2-316)'. By burying the disclaimer within the definitions, it fails to provide the clear exclusion required by law, thus creating a legal contradiction.
